passages.explanation: Paul and his companions sail from Miletus through Cos, Rhodes, and Patara, then board a ship crossing to Phoenicia. They sight Cyprus, pass it on the left, and land at Tyre. Finding the disciples there, they stay seven days. Through the Spirit, the believers urge Paul not to go to Jerusalem. When it's time to leave, the whole congregation — men, women, and children — escorts them out of the city. They kneel on the beach and pray, say their farewells, and part. The pattern repeats: the Spirit reveals danger ahead, believers beg Paul not to go, and Paul goes anyway. He's not ignoring the Spirit — the Spirit is warning him what he'll face, not telling him to stay away.

From Tyre they sail to Ptolemais, greet the believers, and stay a day. Then they go to Caesarea and stay at the house of Philip the evangelist — one of the seven chosen in chapter 6. Philip has four unmarried daughters who prophesy. After several days, a prophet named Agabus comes down from Judea. He takes Paul's belt, ties his own hands and feet, and declares: the Holy Spirit says that in Jerusalem the Jews will bind the owner of this belt and hand him over to the Gentiles. Everyone — both the locals and Paul's companions — begs him not to go. Paul answers: why are you weeping and breaking my heart? I'm ready not only to be bound but to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus. When he won't be persuaded, they give up and say: the Lord's will be done. Paul's resolve is absolute, and his friends accept it with grief.

They pack up and go to Jerusalem. Some disciples from Caesarea accompany them and bring them to stay with Mnason of Cyprus, an early disciple. In Jerusalem, the believers receive them warmly. The next day Paul meets with James and all the elders and reports in detail what God has done among the Gentiles through his ministry. They praise God — but then raise a concern. Thousands of Jews have believed, and they're all zealous for the law. They've heard that Paul teaches Jews living among Gentiles to abandon Moses, not to circumcise their children, or follow Jewish customs. What should be done? They propose that Paul join four men who are completing a purification vow, pay their expenses, and go to the temple with them. This will show everyone that Paul himself still lives in obedience to the law. Paul agrees and begins the purification process the next day. It's a diplomatic compromise — Paul isn't abandoning his theology, but he's demonstrating that he's not anti-Jewish.

When the seven days of purification are nearly completed, Jews from the province of Asia see Paul in the temple. They stir up the whole crowd and seize him, shouting: this is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against our people, our law, and this place! And he's brought Greeks into the temple and defiled this holy place! They'd seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with Paul and assumed Paul had brought him into the temple — which he hadn't. The whole city is aroused. They drag Paul out of the temple, and the gates are shut behind him. They're trying to kill him when the commander of the Roman garrison gets word that all Jerusalem is in uproar. He comes with soldiers and centurions, arrests Paul, orders him bound with two chains, and tries to find out who he is and what he's done. The crowd is so violent the soldiers have to carry Paul up the steps of the barracks. The crowd follows, shouting: away with him!

As Paul is being brought into the barracks, he speaks to the commander in Greek: may I say something to you? The commander is surprised: you speak Greek? Aren't you the Egyptian who started a revolt and led four thousand terrorists into the wilderness? Paul replies: I'm a Jew from Tarsus in Cilicia, a citizen of no ordinary city. Please let me speak to the people. The commander gives permission. Paul stands on the steps, motions to the crowd, and a great hush falls. He begins to speak to them in Aramaic. Paul is about to defend himself — not in a Roman court, but before the Jewish mob that wants him dead.
